input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation is built on Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) practices, employing SysML for multi‑level abstraction (requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers). Architectural models use component‑and‑connector ADLs such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML internal block diagrams (IBDs) to define components, interfaces, and compositions. Behavioral specifications are captured with state machines in SysML v1.7, MontiArc Automaton, or external simulation environments like MathWorks Simulink. The generated or interfaced software may be written in general‑purpose languages such as C++ or Java. These tools together enable the active components required for a Digital Twin to ingest, aggregate, and act upon contextual data traces as defined in the digital‑shadow reference model.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin leverages Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering artefacts: SysML diagrams (including internal block diagrams) for requirements, functional, logical and product layers; architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc and their component‑connector meta‑models; behavioral specifications via state‑machine extensions (e.g., MontiArc Automaton, SysML v1.7 state machines); analytical and simulation models implemented in MathWorks Simulink; and executable code in C++ or Java. These models constitute geometric, logical and behavioral representations that are linked through explicit composition and connector relationships, providing the data foundation for the DT constellation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering of the Digital Twin follows a Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) approach performed during the development phase. System structure and behavior are captured with SysML (including block definition diagrams and internal block diagrams) and complemented by architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc. The development methodology references the RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) framework, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT to handle abstraction levels from requirements through functional, logical, and product/technical layers. Behavioral models are refined using state machines (SysML v1.7) or MontiArc Automaton and linked to simulation tools like MATLAB/Simulink or other domain‑specific simulators. Implementation code may be generated in C++ or Java. Although the document does not explicitly discuss quality‑assurance, testing, or version‑control practices, the use of formal modeling languages and defined methodologies provides traceability and verification mechanisms within the MBSE workflow.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
